attributes.stem:
A 17-year-old girl presents to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of left wrist pain and swelling after a fall onto an outstretched hand. The pain is mainly around the base of the thumb and is worse with gripping. She can still use the hand and has continued working as a teacher. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is localised tenderness in the hollow between the extensor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendons, and over the volar aspect of the radial side of the wrist. Wrist range of motion is mildly painful at the extremes but preserved. Neurovascular status is intact.

A wrist X-ray is performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
The X-ray shows a non-displaced fracture line through the waist of the scaphoid. Scaphoid fractures have a risk of non-union and avascular necrosis due to a retrograde blood supply, particularly at the waist and proximal pole. Initial management decisions balance union rates, complications, time to return to function, and patient factors. Non-displaced waist fractures generally heal well with immobilisation; surgery offers faster radiographic union and earlier return to some activities but carries procedural risks without clear long-term functional superiority for most people.

Below-elbow cast immobilisation is appropriate for a non-displaced scaphoid waist fracture. The key principle is achieving adequate immobilisation at the wrist to restrict movement at the radio-carpal joint. Studies have shown no significant difference in non-union rates between short-arm casts and thumb spica casts, making either option acceptable. However, short-arm casts typically have less functional impact and are better tolerated by patients. This patient would benefit from cast immobilisation with orthopaedic follow-up and repeat imaging (often magnetic resonance imaging or computed tomography at two weeks) to confirm stability and assess healing. As this patient's fracture is not impeding her everyday activities, this conservative approach would be the most appropriate management plan to discuss with her.

Wrist strapping provides inadequate immobilisation of the scaphoid and risks displacement or non-union. Given snuffbox tenderness and a visible waist fracture line, rigid immobilisation is required rather than supportive taping.

Cylinder splint immobilises fingers or the forearm in flexion-extension planes but does not reliably control wrist motion or thumb movement. It is insufficient for scaphoid stability compared with a dedicated below-elbow cast.

Percutaneous compression screw fixation may be considered for athletes or manual workers prioritising rapid return to function, or in fractures showing instability features such as displacement, comminution, or a humpback deformity. In a non-displaced fracture in a person whose work does not demand high wrist load, the operative risks usually outweigh the small time-to-union benefit.

Open reduction with plate fixation is generally reserved for complex, comminuted, or malunited scaphoid fractures, or for salvage after non-union. It is not indicated for an acute, non-displaced waist fracture.
learningPoints: Suspected scaphoid fracture with anatomical snuffbox tenderness warrants immobilisation even if initial radiographs appear normal.
Non-displaced scaphoid waist fractures are usually managed with below-elbow cast immobilisation and early follow-up imaging.
Undertreatment risks non-union and avascular necrosis due to the scaphoid's retrograde blood supply.
references: Royal Children’s Hospital Melbourne. Clinical practice guidelines: scaphoid fractures – emergency management. Available at: https://www.rch.org.au/clinicalguide/guideline_index/fractures/Scaphoid_Fractures_%E2%80%93_Emergency_Management/
Berber O, Ahmad I, Gidwani S. Fractures of the scaphoid. BMJ 2020;369:m1908. Available at: https://www.bmj.com/content/369/bmj.m1908
Fowler JR, Hughes TB. Scaphoid fractures. Clin Sports Med 2015;34:37–50. Available at: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0278591914000919
Grewal R. Surgery versus cast immobilisation for acute scaphoid fractures. Lancet 2020;396:362-63. Available at: https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736(20)30908-7
